speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome to episode 51 of Git Going with GitHub: Git Security for Contributors. I am Alex, and today we are teaching this topic as a conversation you can carry into the workshop, not as a page you have to memorize.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I will keep pulling the lesson back to real learners, real repositories, and the evidence that proves the work happened.

speaker: ALEX
text: The lesson focus is Secrets,.gitignore, environment variables, push protection, and safe contributor habits. We will treat every step as a teachable decision, because that is what makes the skill portable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So we should explain the why clearly enough that the steps make sense when the learner meets them later.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is it. If a listener only has audio right now, they should still get the complete concept and know what evidence would prove success.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: Keeping Secrets Out of Your Repository: Who this is for: Anyone committing code or documentation to a repository. The next useful detail is concrete: You don't need to be a security expert -- this appendix covers the practical habits that protect you and the projects you contribute to.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Learning Cards: Using This Security Reference has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the anchors worth keeping. Sections are ordered from understanding (section 1) to prevention (2-5) to recovery (6-7) to daily habits (8-9). Code blocks contain exact gitignore patterns and terminal commands -- switch to Focus Mode before copying. The Security Checklist (section 9) is a task list you can use before every push. Code examples for.gitignore patterns and terminal commands are in high-contrast code blocks. Warning callouts use bold text -- scan for bold to find the most critical safety notes. The Security Checklist at the bottom uses checkbox formatting for easy visual tracking.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 1. Why This Matters -- What Happens When Secrets Leak: when a secret (API key, token, password, private key) is committed to a public GitHub repository -- even for a few seconds before you delete it -- it's effectively compromised. That matters in practice: Why "I'll just delete it right away" isn't enough.

speaker: ALEX
text: That shows up in the workshop in a few specific ways. Bots scan GitHub continuously and harvest secrets within seconds of a push. The secret lives in your git history even after you delete the file. GitHub forks capture history -- once forked, you can't fully erase it. Search engines may index the content before you remove it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Anchor this part on 2. The.gitignore File -- Your First Line of Defense. A.gitignore file tells Git which files to never track. This is the part to say slowly: Files listed in.gitignore won't show up in git status, won't be staged by git add, and won't be committed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should they understand before typing anything?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple.gitignore only prevents untracked files from being added. The listener should be able to check this: If Git is already tracking a file,.gitignore won't stop it from being committed in the future.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Check if a specific file is tracked; git ls-files.env; If it returns the filename, it's being tracked -- you need to untrack it; git rm --cached.env; Then add it to.gitignore and commit. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you keep commands from becoming magic words?

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat Global.gitignore -- apply to every repo on your machine as decoration. You can create a global.gitignore that applies to all repositories on your computer -- useful for OS-specific and editor-specific files you never want to commit anywhere. That is not trivia. Add your editor and OS files to /.gitignore global so you never have to add them to individual repos. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Create a global gitignore file; touch /.gitignore global; Tell Git to use it; git config --global core.excludesfile /.gitignore global. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the safe way to learn from that example?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, GitHub's.gitignore templates is still useful because when creating a new repository on GitHub, you can choose a.gitignore template for your language -- GitHub pre-fills it with the most common patterns for that ecosystem. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Find all templates at github.com/github/gitignore.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Download a template (e.g., for Node.js); curl https://raw.githubusercontent.com/github/gitignore/main/Node.gitignore.gitignore. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Put 3. Environment Variables -- The Right Way to Store Secrets into plain language. Instead of hardcoding secrets in your files, store them in environment variables that live outside of your repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. A.env file stores your local environment variables. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: It's convenient and universally supported -- and it must be in your.gitignore.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before the learner moves on. This part earns its place because never send secrets in Slack, email, or GitHub comments. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: That becomes easier when you listen for these cues. GitHub Actions Secrets -- for CI/CD pipelines: Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions. A password manager with sharing (1Password Teams, Bitwarden) -- for team credentials. A secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault) -- for production systems.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the pre-flight check here?

speaker: ALEX
text: 4. Review Before You Commit: The most effective habit is simply reviewing what you're about to commit before you commit it.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should happen before anyone copies and runs it?

speaker: ALEX
text: git diff --staged -- see exactly what's going in has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part that makes the next action easier. Any hardcoded passwords, tokens, or API keys.env or credential files that snuck in. Any TODO comments that reference sensitive information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Review all staged changes before committing; git diff --staged; Review a specific file; git diff --staged docs/config.md. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Avoid git add. blindly. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Avoid git add. blindly: stages everything in your working directory -- including files you didn't mean to add. That matters in practice: git add -p (patch mode) walks you through each change chunk by chunk and asks whether to stage it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Risky - stages everything without review; git add.; Better - stage specific files you know are clean; git add src/auth.js docs/README.md; Or stage interactively - review each file before adding; git add -p. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid Git habit is to know which branch you are on, what changed, and what confirmation you expect before you run the next command.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Check what's staged before committing. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Anchor this part on Check what's staged before committing. GitHub Copilot can help: After staging your changes, open Copilot Chat and ask: "Review my staged changes for any accidentally included secrets, API keys, or credentials." Paste the output of git diff --staged into the chat. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like See which files are staged (and which aren't); git status; See the full diff of staged changes; git diff --staged. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: a pre-commit hook is a script that runs automatically every time you try to commit. The listener should be able to check this: If the script detects a problem (like a potential secret), it blocks the commit and tells you what it found.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. Do not treat Option A: detect-secrets (recommended, Python-based) as decoration. detect-secrets scans for over 20 types of secrets and integrates well with existing repos. That is not trivia. After setup, any commit containing a potential secret is blocked with a clear message showing which file and line triggered the alert.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Option C: pre-commit framework (manages multiple hooks) is still useful because the pre-commit framework lets you install and manage hooks from a YAML config file, making it easy to share hook config across your team. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Pre-commit hooks live in.git/hooks/ and are local to your machine -- they're not committed to the repo automatically.

speaker: ALEX
text: Hold that next to this. Put Step 1: Rotate the secret immediately into plain language. Before anything else -- go to wherever that secret is managed and revoke or rotate it. The useful version is: It may already be compromised, so neutralizing it is more important than removing it from git history. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Step 2: Was it pushed to a public repo? What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. If it was pushed (remote has the secret). That is the difference between guessing and knowing: The secret is potentially already compromised -- assume it was harvested.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Undo the last commit, keep your changes staged (safest); git reset --soft HEAD 1; Now remove the secret from the file, re-add, and re-commit; (Edit the file to remove the secret); git add -p Review what you stage; git commit -m "Your original commit message. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: That connects to another useful point. This part earns its place because this only matters if the commit was pushed. That gives the learner a foothold: if it was local-only and you used git reset --soft above, you're done.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Method A: git filter-repo (recommended -- built-in, modern). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Method A: git filter-repo (recommended -- built-in, modern) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Install git-filter-repo; pip install git-filter-repo; Remove a specific file from all history; git filter-repo --path secrets.json --invert-paths; Replace a specific string (the secret value) throughout all history; git filter-repo --replace-text REMOVED"). Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Step 4: Force push the cleaned history. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the learner-facing version. After rewriting history, you must force push. Put another way, anyone who has cloned or pulled the repo will need to re-clone or rebase after a force push. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git push --force-with-lease origin main. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Step 5: Tell GitHub to rescan: after removing the secret from history, go to Security → Secret scanning in your repository and mark any open alerts as resolved.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Anchor this part on 7. GitHub's Built-In Push Protection. GitHub automatically scans pushes for known secret patterns before they reach the remote. This is the part to say slowly: If it detects a secret, the push is blocked.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on What push protection covers. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: What push protection covers has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. GitHub tokens (PATs, GitHub App tokens, OAuth tokens). AWS access keys. Azure credentials. Google Cloud keys. Stripe, Twilio, Slack, and dozens more API keys.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: If push protection blocks you has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, confirm it's actually a secret -- check the file and line mentioned. Then, if it's a real secret: Remove it from the file, amend your commit, and push again. After that, if it's a false positive: Use the bypass URL GitHub provides to push with an explanation. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Checking your repo's push protection status. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Checking your repo's push protection status is still useful because as a contributor you can see push protection in action when a push is blocked. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Maintainers configure it in Settings → Code security → Push protection.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Never store credentials in plaintext. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Put Never store credentials in plaintext into plain language. Do this instead - use the OS credential store. The useful version is: With a credential helper set, Git asks for your credentials once and stores them securely in the OS keychain -- not in any file.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Storing a token in a plain text file; echo "ghp mytoken" /token.txt; Hardcoding in a script; export GITHUB TOKEN="ghp mytoken" in a.bashrc or.zshrc that's committed; In a git config; git config --global url."https://myusername:ghp mytoken@github.com".insteadOf. macOS -- use Keychain; git config --global credential.helper osxkeychain; Windows -- use Credential Manager (set automatically by Git for Windows); git config --global credential.helper wincred; Linux -- use the libsecret store (requires installation); git. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Using a password manager. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Store your GitHub PAT, SSH key passphrase, and other credentials in a password manager (1Password, Bitwarden, KeePass). That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Most support browser extensions, CLI access, and automatic lock after inactivity.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Checking what credential helper is set. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This part earns its place because if this returns nothing, your credentials may be stored in plaintext. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git config --global credential.helper. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Security Checklist. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Security Checklist has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. The checklist below uses Markdown task list formatting -- each item is announced as "checkbox not checked". Items are grouped into three categories: Before Committing, Before Pushing, and Repository Setup. Read through the list once to learn the habits, then use it as a pre-push routine. Checkboxes create a clear visual pattern for scanning -- each line starts with a square box. Three groups are separated by h3 headings: Before Committing, Before Pushing, Repository Setup. Consider copying this checklist into a personal note and checking items off for each project.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now shift from knowing the term to using it. Here is the learner-facing version. Use this before every push to a public repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does someone need before they touch the keyboard?

speaker: ALEX
text: Before committing has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: A few details make that real. [ ] I reviewed git diff --staged and didn't see any tokens, passwords, or keys. [ ] I used git add or git add -p rather than git add. [ ] Any.env files or credential files are listed in.gitignore. [ ] Config files with real values are in.gitignore; only example/template files are committed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the part I would want someone to say out loud while they work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A learner should always know what they are trying to prove before they take the next action.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where confidence starts to build. Before pushing has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. [ ] git log --oneline -5 -- all commits look expected. [ ] No commits with messages like "remove secret" or "oops" that suggest a secret was added and removed (the secret is still in history).

speaker: JAMIE
text: If setup starts to feel like a barrier, how should a learner think about it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Repository setup (one time) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. [ ].gitignore includes.env,.key,.pem, and relevant patterns for your stack. [ ] Global.gitignore ( /.gitignore global) covers editor/OS files. [ ] Git credential helper is configured to use the OS keychain. [ ] (Optional) A pre-commit hook is installed to scan for secrets automatically.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Do not treat If you're a maintainer as decoration. See also: Appendix L: GitHub Security Features for the GitHub platform security tools (Dependabot, secret scanning alerts, code scanning). That is not trivia. Appendix D: Git Authentication for SSH keys, PATs, and commit signing.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. [ ] Branch protection is enabled on main with required reviews and status checks. [ ] Secret scanning is enabled (Settings → Code security → Secret scanning). [ ] Push protection is enabled for the repository. [ ] A SECURITY.md file exists with instructions for reporting vulnerabilities.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 51. Next in the series is episode 52, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
